does: This paper introduces DisaBench, a participatory evaluation framework specifically designed to assess disability-related harms in large language models. The authors develop a taxonomy of twelve disability harm categories through co-creation with people with disabilities and red teaming experts, create a methodology that pairs benign and adversarial prompts across seven life domains, and compile a dataset of 175 prompts with human annotations from 525 prompt-response pairs. Key findings reveal that harm rates vary significantly by disability type and are expected to compound in non-text modalities, that terminology-driven harm is culturally and temporally situated rather than universal, and that standard safety evaluation approaches miss subtle harms that only domain experts with lived experience can identify. The work demonstrates that disability harm is fundamentally personal, intersectional, and community-defined, requiring contextual understanding beyond isolated evaluation metrics.
connects: This work extends recent efforts to move beyond generic safety benchmarks toward more nuanced, community-centered AI evaluation practices. It builds on participatory design approaches in AI fairness and safety research that recognize how vulnerable communities are systematically underrepresented in standard benchmarks. The paper responds to limitations of existing safety evaluation frameworks like those used for general harmful content detection, which tend to focus on explicit, obvious failures rather than subtle contextual harms. It connects to broader disability justice literature and intersectionality frameworks that emphasize how impacts of technology cannot be separated from lived identity and community context. The research also engages with red teaming methodologies and adversarial prompt generation techniques, adapting these to specifically surface disability-related failure modes that general-purpose benchmarks miss.
enables: This framework opens pathways for disability-centered auditing of language models and enables future safety evaluation to incorporate community expertise systematically. The released dataset, taxonomy, and methodology provide concrete tools for researchers and practitioners to assess models through a disability lens, supporting more equitable model development and deployment practices. The findings suggest that similar participatory evaluation approaches should be applied to other marginalized communities whose needs are systematically overlooked. The work points toward evaluating harms across modalities beyond text, recognizing that disability accessibility requirements will become more complex as multimodal AI systems proliferate. It also enables future investigation into how intersectional factors compound harms across multiple marginalized identities, and supports development of more nuanced safety training and fine-tuning approaches that account for subtle, context-dependent failures rather than just overt harmful outputs.
